description: Design Intelligence Engine — captures the reasoning a CTS-D designer uses when speccing systems. Organized by discipline (audio, video, networking, security) with environmental factors that modify every calculation.

environmentalFactors.description: These are the questions the agent MUST ask or infer before it can design anything. A floor plan alone is not enough.

ceilingHeight.impact: Determines speaker model selection, mounting type, coverage geometry, display mounting, camera angles

medium.notes: May need pendant speakers or high-output models, extended drop mounts for displays

high.notes: Pendant or high-output speakers required, structural mounting considerations

veryHigh.notes: Specialized speaker arrays, distributed vs. point-source analysis required

impact.drop-tile-2x2: Standard ceiling speaker cutout, tile-bridge mount, easy cable access
impact.drop-tile-2x4: Standard ceiling speaker cutout, tile-bridge mount, easy cable access
impact.drywall: Requires new-construction ring or retrofit clamp, harder cable routing
impact.exposed-deck: Pendant mount required, longer reverb time, visible cable management needed
impact.open-plenum: Pendant or suspended mount, acoustic treatment likely needed
impact.concrete: Surface mount or pendant, core drilling for cable, structural engineer may be needed
impact.metal-deck: Pendant mount, magnetic or beam clamp attachment, acoustic reflections
impact.outdoor-none: Weather-rated speakers required, wall or pole mount, different coverage calc

ambientNoise.description: Background noise level that the audio system must overcome
ambientNoise.rule: Target SPL should be 10-25dB above ambient depending on use case. Background music = +10dB. Foreground music = +15-20dB. High-energy fitness/nightclub = +20-25dB. Paging/announcement = +15dB.

wallFinishes.impact: Reflective surfaces (glass, concrete, tile, stone) increase reverb and reduce intelligibility. Absorptive surfaces (acoustic panel, curtain, carpet) reduce reverb. Mixed environments need zone-by-zone analysis.

floorFinish.impact: Hard floors add reflections. Carpet absorbs. Fitness centers typically have rubber mat (moderate absorption).

windowCoverage.description: Percentage of wall area that is glass/window
windowCoverage.impact: High glass = high reflections, display glare considerations, camera backlight issues

occupancyType.description: How the space is used — critical for SPL targets and coverage uniformity requirements

adjacencies.description: What's next to this space — sound isolation requirements
examples: fitness next to spa = need sound isolation or volume zoning
ballroom next to meeting rooms = partition sensing, separate zones
bar next to restaurant = volume differential management
conference room next to conference room = speech privacy concerns

optional.existingInfrastructure: What's already in place — conduit, cable paths, rack locations, existing speakers to reuse
optional.budgetGuidance: Client budget range — affects equipment tier selection
optional.futureExpansion: Planned changes to the space — helps size infrastructure
optional.networkExisting: Existing network infrastructure and VLAN availability

speakerSelection.description: Speaker model selection based on environmental factors, not just room type

logic.result: Standard ceiling speaker (e.g., JBL Control 26CT, QSC AD-C6T)
logic.reasoning: Tile bridge mount, standard coverage angle sufficient

logic.condition: ceilingType is drop-tile AND ceilingHeight > 12 AND ceilingHeight <= 16
logic.result: High-output ceiling speaker (e.g., JBL Control 328CT, QSC AD-C820)
logic.reasoning: Higher output needed to maintain SPL at listening plane from increased height

logic.result: Pendant speaker (e.g., JBL Control 65P/T, QSC AD-P6T)
logic.reasoning: No ceiling grid for standard mount. Pendant brings speaker closer to listening plane.

logic.result: High-output pendant or point-source speaker array
logic.reasoning: Standard ceiling speakers cannot achieve 85+ dBA at floor level from this height

logic.result: Weather-rated speaker (e.g., JBL Control 28-1, QSC AD-S6T)
logic.reasoning: IP-rated enclosure required for moisture/UV exposure

coverageCalculation.description: Speaker quantity based on coverage geometry, not just sqft division
coverageCalculation.method: Coverage diameter at listening plane determines spacing

formula.step1: Determine speaker mounting height above listening plane (ceiling height minus 4ft for standing, minus 3ft for seated)
formula.step2: Look up speaker coverage angle from spec sheet (typically 100-150 degrees conical)
formula.step3: Calculate coverage diameter: D = 2 × H × tan(angle/2) where H = mounting height above ear
formula.step4: Apply edge-to-edge overlap of 50% for uniform coverage: spacing = D × 0.707
formula.step5: Grid layout: rows = room_length / spacing, cols = room_width / spacing
formula.step7: Verify SPL: if calculated SPL at listener < target, upgrade to higher output model or reduce spacing

example.mountingHeight: 14ft ceiling, pendant drops 2ft = 12ft mount, minus 5.5ft ear height = 6.5ft above ear
example.note: Compare to naive sqft rule: 2000/250 = 8 speakers. The geometry-based calc says 12 for uniform coverage. The sqft rule would leave dead spots.

splVerification.description: Verify the system can hit target SPL at the listener position

formula.multipleSourcesBoost: +3dB per doubling of speakers in overlap zone (approximate)
formula.amplifierPower: Required watts = 10^((target_SPL - sensitivity) / 10) where sensitivity is at 1W/1m
formula.headroom: Add 6-10dB headroom above target for peaks and dynamic range

foreground-music.notes: Clearly audible, some effort to talk over

speech-reinforcement.notes: Clear intelligibility is priority over volume

amplifierSizing.description: Amp sizing based on actual power requirements, not just speaker count
method: Calculate total watts needed: sum of (per-speaker wattage × number of speakers) per zone
Per-speaker wattage: based on SPL target, speaker sensitivity, distance, headroom
Apply 80% rule: don't run amps above 80% rated power for thermal headroom
Total amp channels needed: typically 1 channel per speaker or zone depending on impedance topology
Select amp: match total watts at impedance (4/8/70V/100V line), channel count, rack space

lowImpedance.when: Small zones with few speakers (1-4), short cable runs (<100ft)
lowImpedance.notes: Simpler, better fidelity, but limited speakers per channel

distributedLine70V.when: Large zones, many speakers, long cable runs, commercial installations
distributedLine70V.notes: Industry standard for commercial AV. Each speaker taps power from line. Cable gauge matters.

distributedLine100V.when: Same as 70V but for international installations or very long runs
distributedLine100V.notes: Higher voltage = less current = smaller cable for same power

danteAoIP.when: Large, complex venues where zones change frequently, premium installations
danteAoIP.notes: Each speaker (or amp) is networked. Most flexible but highest cost and complexity.

dspRequirements.description: DSP needed for EQ, routing, volume control, zone management
rules: Every commercial audio system needs DSP — no exceptions
Minimum: input routing, output EQ, volume control per zone
Add delay processing if speakers at different distances from listener
Add feedback suppression if open microphones (paging, conferencing)
Add automatic gain control for spaces with varying occupancy
Channel count: inputs (sources) + outputs (speaker zones) determines DSP model
Consider Dante/AoIP vs analog connections based on infrastructure

subwooferPlacement.description: Subwoofers have omnidirectional low-frequency output — placement rules differ from speakers
rules: Subs are NOT 1-per-X-speakers. They fill the room's low-frequency content.
Small room (<1000 sqft): 1 sub is usually sufficient
Medium room (1000-3000 sqft): 1-2 subs depending on SPL target
Large room (3000-10000 sqft): 2-4 subs, distributed to avoid standing waves
Very large (>10000 sqft): Distributed sub arrays, requires acoustic modeling
Corner loading: placing sub near a wall boundary gives +3dB, in a corner gives +6dB
Ceiling subs exist for fitness/retail but have lower output than floor subs

displaySizing.description: Display size based on viewing distance and content type

avixa_v201.basicDecision: Viewer can make basic YES/NO decisions. Min character height = viewing distance / 200
avixa_v201.passiveViewing: Digital signage, ambient content. Min display height = viewing distance / 8
avixa_v201.analyticalDecision: Viewer needs to read text, analyze data. Min character height = viewing distance / 100
avixa_v201.inspection: Critical detail viewing (medical, security). Min character height = viewing distance / 50

rules: Maximum viewing distance for a 55" display (basic decision): ~36ft
Maximum viewing distance for a 65" display (basic decision): ~42ft
Maximum viewing distance for a 75" display (basic decision): ~48ft
For 4K content, minimum viewing distance = 1.5× display height for full resolution benefit
Mounting height: center of display at standing eye height (5.5ft) or seated (4ft) ±6 inches
In fitness: mount higher (7-8ft center) for visibility over equipment, but reduce max viewing distance

contentType.description: Content determines resolution and display tier requirements

types.digital-signage: 4K preferred, commercial panel with 500+ nit brightness, 16/7 or 24/7 rated
types.cable-tv-sports: 4K, fast refresh rate, commercial tuner or content player
types.presentation: 4K, accurate color, HDMI/USB-C input, interactive option for meeting rooms
types.menu-board: 4K commercial, portrait orientation support, bright (700+ nit)
types.wayfinding: Touch-enabled, commercial rated, accessible mounting height
types.video-wall: Narrow bezel panels, content management system, dedicated processor

wirelessCoverage.description: AP placement based on density requirements, not just area
rules: Coverage area per AP varies dramatically: 1500-5000 sqft depending on construction and density
High-density (conference, ballroom, lobby): 1 AP per 30-50 users or 1500-2000 sqft
Medium-density (office, restaurant, fitness): 1 AP per 2000-3000 sqft
Low-density (corridor, BOH, parking): 1 AP per 3000-5000 sqft
Wall material attenuation: drywall -3dB, glass -2dB, concrete -15dB, elevator shaft -40dB
Channel planning: max 3 non-overlapping channels on 2.4GHz, 24+ on 5/6GHz
Mount APs at ceiling, centered in coverage zone, away from metal objects

switchSizing.description: Switch sizing based on actual PoE device count and power budget
rules: Count ALL PoE devices: APs, cameras, touch panels, IP speakers, phones, door hardware
PoE budget per device: AP=15-30W, camera=12-25W, touch panel=15W, phone=7W
Total PoE watts determines switch model (150W, 370W, 740W budgets typical)
Add 25% headroom to PoE budget for future devices
Uplink: every access switch needs 10G uplink to distribution/core
Don't forget management ports, AP controller, NVR connections

cameraPlacement.description: Camera placement based on what needs to be SEEN, not just sqft
principles: Identify what you need to capture: face identification, license plate, activity overview, perimeter
IDENTIFICATION requires 80+ pixels per foot — close range, narrow FOV
RECOGNITION requires 40+ pixels per foot — medium range
DETECTION requires 20+ pixels per foot — wide FOV, overview
Match camera resolution and lens to required pixels-per-foot at the target distance
Indoor: domes for aesthetics and vandal resistance
Outdoor: bullets for range, domes for vandal areas, PTZ for large open areas
Lighting: IR illumination range must match or exceed camera view distance
Storage: calculate per camera: resolution × fps × hours × retention days = storage

storageCalculation.formula: Bitrate (Mbps) × 3600 × hours_per_day × retention_days / 8 / 1024 / 1024 = TB per camera

typical.1080p-15fps-h264: ~8 Mbps → ~2.6 TB per camera for 30 days at 24/7
typical.4K-15fps-h265: ~12 Mbps → ~3.9 TB per camera for 30 days at 24/7
typical.4K-30fps-h265: ~20 Mbps → ~6.5 TB per camera for 30 days at 24/7

storageCalculation.notes: Motion-based recording reduces storage 40-70% depending on activity level

designQuestionnaire.description: The agent should collect this information before designing. This is the conversational flow.
phase1_basics: What type of space is this? (room type / use case)
What is the approximate square footage?
What type of ceiling? (drop tile, drywall, exposed, etc.)
phase2_environment: What are the wall finishes? (glass, drywall, concrete, etc.)
What is the floor finish? (carpet, hardwood, rubber, etc.)
Is this a new construction or retrofit?
Any existing infrastructure we can leverage?
phase3_requirements: What is the audio use case? (background music, foreground, paging, presentation, etc.)
What security requirements? (cameras, access control, etc.)
Any adjacency concerns? (noise bleed to neighboring spaces)

designerDrivenMode.description: Designer places equipment. Agent validates, catches gaps, suggests improvements.
triggers: Designer places standard ceiling speakers in a room with 16ft exposed ceiling → Agent: 'Ceiling height is 16ft with exposed deck — standard ceiling speakers won't have sufficient SPL at the listening plane. Consider pendant-mount high-output models.'
Designer has 8 cameras but no NVR → Agent: 'You have 8 cameras specified but no NVR or recording solution. At 1080p/15fps with 30-day retention, you need approximately 21TB of storage.'
Designer specs 12 PoE devices on a switch with 150W budget → Agent: 'Estimated PoE draw is 240W which exceeds the 150W budget on this switch. Consider upgrading to a 370W model.'
Designer has speakers in a spa zone at same volume setting as adjacent fitness → Agent: 'The spa zone is adjacent to fitness. Consider independent volume control and sound masking to prevent bleed.'

agentDrivenMode.description: Agent designs the system. Designer reviews and refines.
flow: 1. Agent receives floor plan + zone definitions
2. Agent asks environmental questions (questionnaire above) for each zone
3. Agent performs coverage calculations using actual geometry, not sqft shortcuts
4. Agent selects equipment based on environmental factors + calculation results
5. Agent sizes infrastructure (amps, DSP, switches, NVR) based on device totals
6. Agent places equipment on canvas with proper spacing
7. Agent presents design with reasoning: 'I selected 12 pendant speakers because...'
9. Agent generates proposal with design notes and standards citations
